front: Name the OWASP Top 10 (2021) categories and the one category that causes the most critical vulnerabilities.
back: OWASP Top 10 (2021): A01 Broken Access Control (moved to #1 — most frequent critical finding), A02 Cryptographic Failures (weak/missing encryption), A03 Injection (SQLi, XSS, command injection), A04 Insecure Design (missing threat modeling), A05 Security Misconfiguration, A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components, A07 Identification and Authentication Failures, A08 Software and Data Integrity Failures (insecure deserialization, CI/CD pipeline tampering), A09 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures, A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF — new in 2021). A01 Broken Access Control rose from #5 in 2017 to #1 because it is systemic: horizontal privilege escalation (accessing another user's data by changing an ID) and vertical escalation (accessing admin functions) are found in the vast majority of application penetration tests.
senior_nuance: A08 (Software and Data Integrity) encompasses supply chain attacks (SolarWinds, npm dependency confusion). A09 (Logging failures) is not an attack vector — it is the absence of the ability to detect and respond to attacks. The Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) vulnerability would be classified under A06 (Outdated Components) and A03 (Injection via JNDI lookup in log messages).
quote_to_say: Broken access control is #1 because authorization is easy to skip during feature development.

front: How does SQL injection work, and why do parameterized queries prevent it completely?
back: SQL injection: user input is concatenated directly into a SQL string. Example: `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '` + input + `'`. If input = `' OR '1'='1`, the query becomes `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '' OR '1'='1'` — returns all rows. Worse: `'; DROP TABLE users; --`. Parameterized queries (prepared statements) fix this by sending the query structure and the data separately to the database driver. The driver transmits the parameter as a data value, never as SQL syntax — the database engine never parses it as code. No escaping, no sanitization, no allowlist — just structural separation. ORMs that use query builders (not raw string interpolation) also prevent injection as long as raw query methods (`query()`, `execute()`) are not used with interpolated strings.
senior_nuance: Second-order SQLi is subtler: user input is stored safely (parameterized), then later retrieved and used in another query without parameterization. Blind SQLi (no output returned) exploits time-based or boolean-based differences (SLEEP(5) if condition is true). Second-order and blind injection are not caught by basic code review — require dynamic testing (sqlmap) and data flow analysis. ORM 'raw' methods are the main source of injection in modern apps.
quote_to_say: Parameterized queries separate code from data — the database never confuses user input for SQL.

front: Distinguish stored, reflected, and DOM-based XSS. What does Content-Security-Policy actually prevent?
back: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) injects malicious scripts into pages viewed by other users. Stored (persistent) XSS: malicious script is saved to the database (e.g., in a comment), then served to every visitor who loads that page — highest impact, affects all users. Reflected XSS: script is embedded in the URL, reflected in the response but not stored — requires the victim to click a crafted link. DOM-based XSS: JavaScript reads attacker-controlled data (URL fragment, localStorage) and writes it to innerHTML without sanitization — no server involvement, invisible to server-side WAFs. Prevention: output encoding (HTML-encode all user content before rendering), use template engines that auto-escape, use textContent instead of innerHTML, validate/sanitize input. CSP (Content-Security-Policy): a response header that tells the browser which script sources are allowed. A strict CSP (`script-src 'self'`) blocks inline scripts and scripts from other origins — an XSS payload of `<script>evil()</script>` is blocked even if injected. CSP does not fix XSS but reduces its exploitability.
senior_nuance: Trusted Types (Chrome API) enforces that DOM sinks (innerHTML, eval) only accept sanitized objects, not raw strings — moves the enforcement into the browser at the API level. Angular and React prevent most XSS by default via their template engines, but both have escape hatches (dangerouslySetInnerHTML, bypassSecurityTrustHtml) that are common audit targets. The nonce-based CSP (`script-src 'nonce-<random>'`) is the gold standard — each script tag gets a per-request nonce, preventing injected static scripts.
quote_to_say: Stored XSS affects every future visitor; reflected needs the victim to click a link — both need output encoding.

front: Explain CSRF. Why does the SameSite cookie attribute make CSRF tokens largely unnecessary?
back: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery): an attacker tricks an authenticated user's browser into sending a state-changing request to a legitimate site. The browser automatically includes cookies with every request — including forged ones from a malicious page. Classic defense: CSRF token — a secret random value tied to the session, embedded in forms, verified server-side. The attacker cannot read the token (same-origin policy prevents reading cross-origin responses) and cannot forge it. SameSite cookie attribute: tells browsers not to send the cookie in cross-site requests. SameSite=Strict: cookie sent only for same-site requests (breaks OAuth flows). SameSite=Lax (browser default since Chrome 80): cookie sent for same-site requests and top-level GET navigations, but NOT for cross-site form POSTs, iframes, or AJAX — blocks the vast majority of CSRF attacks. SameSite=None requires Secure flag and sends the cookie everywhere (needed for embedded widgets). With Lax as default, CSRF tokens are now defense-in-depth rather than the primary control.
senior_nuance: CSRF attacks target state-changing requests (POST/PUT/DELETE), not reads. SameSite=Lax does not protect custom HTTP methods routed via GET (antipattern but exists). Double-submit cookie (setting a CSRF token as both a cookie and a request header) works because scripts on another origin cannot set cookies for your domain. The Origin header (sent for non-GET cross-origin requests) is a simpler check than CSRF tokens — verify Origin === expected on state-changing endpoints.
quote_to_say: SameSite=Lax made CSRF tokens optional — the browser enforces the policy now.

front: What is SSRF, and how does it enable cloud metadata exfiltration? What is the correct defense?
back: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery): an attacker controls a URL that the server fetches on their behalf, causing the server to make requests to internal infrastructure the attacker cannot reach directly. Example: a webhook URL of `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/role-name` fetches AWS EC2 instance metadata, returning temporary AWS credentials — full account compromise. Common attack surfaces: URL preview/unfurl features, PDF generators that render remote images, XML parsers with external entity resolution (XXE), webhook endpoints, image import from URL. Correct defense: allowlist of approved external domains/IPs (deny by default). Also: block requests to RFC 1918 addresses (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x), link-local (169.254.x.x), loopback (127.x), and cloud metadata IPs. Validate the resolved IP after DNS resolution (DNS rebinding attack bypasses hostname checks). AWS IMDSv2 requires a PUT request to obtain a session token before metadata is accessible — mitigates SSRF against the metadata endpoint.
senior_nuance: DNS rebinding SSRF bypass: the attacker controls a DNS record that first resolves to an external IP (passing the hostname allowlist check), then quickly re-resolves to 169.254.169.254 when the server makes the actual connection. Defense: resolve the hostname once, validate the IP, then use the IP directly for the connection. SSRF is particularly dangerous in cloud environments because metadata endpoints are reachable from any instance without authentication.
quote_to_say: SSRF lets the attacker use your server as a proxy into your internal network.

front: Why is bcrypt better than SHA-256 for password hashing? Why is argon2id better than bcrypt?
back: SHA-256 (and MD5, SHA-1) is designed to be fast — millions of operations per second on commodity hardware. For passwords this is catastrophic: an attacker with a leaked hash database can brute-force billions of candidates per second. Bcrypt is a password KDF (key derivation function) with a cost factor (work factor): it deliberately requires O(2^cost) iterations, making each guess expensive. At cost=12, bcrypt takes ~250ms per hash — acceptable for login, prohibitive for brute force. Bcrypt limitation: fixed 72-byte input limit (silently truncates longer passwords), uses a Blowfish-based algorithm that maps poorly to GPU parallelism. Argon2id (RFC 9106, 2021 winner of Password Hashing Competition) adds: configurable memory hardness (memory parameter, e.g., 64MB) — GPUs have limited VRAM per core, forcing fewer parallel guesses; time cost and parallelism parameters. Memory-hard algorithms make GPU/ASIC attacks 10-1000× less effective. Argon2id = time + memory + resistance to side-channel attacks (id variant).
senior_nuance: OWASP recommends argon2id with m=19456 (19MB), t=2 iterations, p=1 parallelism as minimum (2023). Bcrypt at cost=10-12 remains acceptable for legacy systems. scrypt is memory-hard too but has parameter interactions that make safe configuration harder. Never use PBKDF2 for new systems — it is time-hard only, GPU-friendly. Always store the full hash string including algorithm, version, and parameters (e.g., `$argon2id$v=19$m=65536,t=3,p=4$...`) so future re-hashing on login is possible.
quote_to_say: bcrypt slows brute force; argon2id also constrains GPU parallelism via memory hardness.

front: Distinguish symmetric and asymmetric cryptography. Why does TLS use both?
back: Symmetric encryption: the same key encrypts and decrypts. Fast (~1-10 GB/s on hardware AES-NI). Problem: key distribution — how do two parties share a secret key securely over an insecure channel? Examples: AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305. Asymmetric (public-key) encryption: mathematically linked key pair — public key encrypts (or verifies signatures), private key decrypts (or signs). Slow (~1-10 MB/s for RSA, faster for ECDH). Solves key distribution: publish the public key openly, keep private key secret. Examples: RSA, ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman), Ed25519. TLS uses both: asymmetric crypto in the handshake to authenticate the server (certificate + signature) and establish a shared secret (Diffie-Hellman key exchange). Once both sides derive the same shared secret, symmetric encryption (AES-GCM or ChaCha20) is used for all application data — at least 100× faster than asymmetric for bulk data.
senior_nuance: ECDH (Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman) does not encrypt anything — it is a key agreement protocol. Both parties compute the same shared secret from each other's public key without ever transmitting the secret. The certificate (RSA/ECDSA signature) proves the server owns the key — without it, anyone could perform ECDH and impersonate the server. This separation (key agreement + authentication) is fundamental to TLS 1.3's design.
quote_to_say: Asymmetric crypto solves key distribution; symmetric solves bulk performance — TLS needs both.

front: How does PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) work, and what happens if a CA is compromised?
back: PKI establishes a chain of trust. A Certificate Authority (CA) is a trusted entity that signs digital certificates, binding a public key to an identity (domain name, organization). Chain: Root CA (self-signed, offline) → Intermediate CA (signed by root) → End-entity certificate (signed by intermediate). Browsers ship a list of ~150 trusted root CAs (the root store). A TLS connection presents the end-entity cert + intermediate chain. The browser verifies the chain up to a trusted root, checks the domain matches, and verifies the signature. If a CA is compromised (DigiNotar 2011, Comodo 2011): an attacker can issue fraudulent certificates for any domain — enabling MITM attacks that pass TLS validation. Mitigations: Certificate Transparency (CT) — all publicly trusted certificates must be logged in append-only CT logs; browsers reject certs not in the log. CAA DNS record — specifies which CAs may issue certs for a domain. Certificate pinning — hardcode expected cert/public key hash in the client (risky for longevity).
senior_nuance: DigiNotar's compromise led to fraudulent Google certificates used to MITM Iranian users. The incident destroyed DigiNotar and accelerated Certificate Transparency adoption. CT logs are append-only and publicly auditable — domain owners can monitor logs for unauthorized issuance. Let's Encrypt ships all certificates to CT logs by default. OCSP stapling (server includes a signed proof of cert validity in the TLS handshake) avoids OCSP privacy leakage and the soft-fail security issue of browser OCSP checks.
quote_to_say: Certificate Transparency makes CA misbehavior publicly auditable — the CT log is the accountability layer.

front: Name three critical JWT security pitfalls and the correct defense for each.
back: 1. alg:none attack: the JWT header specifies the signature algorithm. If the server accepts 'none', an attacker removes the signature entirely and sets alg:none — the server trusts an unsigned token. Fix: explicitly allowlist accepted algorithms (e.g., only HS256 or RS256); never accept 'none'. 2. Algorithm confusion (RS256 to HS256): when a server uses RS256 (asymmetric), it verifies with the public key. If the server also accepts HS256, an attacker takes the server's public key (often publicly available) and uses it as the HMAC secret to forge a valid HS256 token. Fix: do not accept both asymmetric and symmetric algorithms for the same key; enforce algorithm from server config, never from the token header. 3. Sub/subject confusion: if a service issues tokens where sub is the user ID, and another service trusts tokens from the first service but uses sub for a different semantic (e.g., as an admin role check), token reuse across services leads to privilege escalation. Fix: validate iss (issuer) and aud (audience) claims; reject tokens not issued for your service.
senior_nuance: JWT expiration (exp claim) is only as safe as the server's clock sync and its willingness to reject expired tokens. Stateless JWTs cannot be revoked before expiry — the standard mitigations are short expiry (5-15min) with refresh tokens, or maintaining a server-side token blocklist (sacrificing statelessness). The latter is essentially session management with extra steps — choose the right abstraction for your threat model.
quote_to_say: Never trust the algorithm from the token header — only accept what your server expects.

front: What is a timing attack on HMAC comparison, and how does constant-time comparison fix it?
back: A naive string comparison (`a == b`) returns early as soon as a mismatching byte is found — the time taken reveals how many bytes matched at the prefix. An attacker who can measure request latency (even over the network with enough samples) can recover a secret byte-by-byte: send candidates for byte 0 and time responses — the correct candidate takes slightly longer because it matches one more byte before failing. Applied to HMAC: if the server compares a computed MAC to the provided MAC with early-exit comparison, an attacker can forge a valid MAC by recovering it one byte at a time. Constant-time comparison: compare all bytes unconditionally, accumulating differences in a variable, and return a single result at the end. In code: Python `hmac.compare_digest()`, Go `subtle.ConstantTimeCompare()`, libsodium `crypto_verify_32()`. Clock resolution mitigates but does not eliminate this attack — sufficient samples overcome noise.
senior_nuance: Timing attacks extend beyond MAC comparison: password comparison (hash both sides, then constant-time compare hashes), cache-timing attacks on AES S-box lookups (the basis of AES cache-timing attacks on non-AES-NI implementations), and branch-timing attacks in RSA (Bleichenbacher's padding oracle, Lucky Thirteen against TLS CBC mode). Constant-time programming is a discipline — any data-dependent branch or memory access can leak secrets. Cryptographic libraries (libsodium, BoringSSL) are written with this constraint throughout.
quote_to_say: A MAC comparison that exits early leaks the secret one byte at a time — compare everything, always.

front: What is certificate pinning, what attack does it prevent, and what is its operational cost?
back: Certificate pinning: a client hardcodes the expected TLS certificate or public key (its hash, called SPKI hash) for a server. On connection, the client verifies the presented certificate matches the pinned value — rejects connections to servers presenting valid but unexpected certificates. Prevents: MITM attacks using a fraudulent certificate signed by a compromised or complicit CA (rogue CA attack, enterprise proxy interception, nation-state MITM). Used in mobile apps (Android/iOS), high-security APIs. Operational cost: when the server's certificate is renewed (or the key rotated after a compromise), every client must be updated or it will reject the connection. A failed pin update breaks the app for all users until they update. Mitigations: pin the CA or intermediate cert instead of the leaf (longer lifetime), provide backup pins, ship certificate update APIs. HPKP (HTTP Public Key Pinning header) was deprecated and removed from browsers — too many sites bricked themselves with misconfigured pins.
senior_nuance: Certificate Transparency (CT) provides a softer, more robust alternative to pinning: instead of rejecting connections to unexpected certs, CT ensures all issued certs are publicly logged and can be monitored. Pinning is appropriate for mobile apps shipping with a fixed binary and known server infrastructure. It is inappropriate for browsers or any client that cannot guarantee fast certificate update propagation.
quote_to_say: Pinning prevents rogue CA attacks but makes certificate rotation an operational emergency.

front: Beyond SQL injection, what other injection classes exist and what is the unifying defense principle?
back: Injection classes: (1) SQL injection — user input interpreted as SQL syntax. (2) Command injection — input passed to shell (os.system, exec, popen with shell=True) — `; rm -rf /`. (3) LDAP injection — input in LDAP queries for auth bypass. (4) XPath injection — input in XPath queries. (5) Template injection (SSTI) — input rendered by a server-side template engine (Jinja2 `{{7*7}}`, Twig). (6) Log injection — CRLF sequences in logs to forge log entries. (7) XML/XXE — external entity references in XML parsing. (8) Header injection — CRLF in user-controlled HTTP headers. Unifying defense: structural separation of code and data. Send structure separately from data to the interpreter (parameterized queries, subprocess with argument lists not shell strings, disabling external entities in XML parsers). Context-aware output encoding as a second layer. Allowlist validation (e.g., only accept alphanumeric filenames) as a third layer. Sanitization/escaping alone is fragile — a single missed escaping call creates the vulnerability.
senior_nuance: SSTI (Server-Side Template Injection) is frequently underestimated — Jinja2 with `{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}` can achieve RCE. The detection method is injecting `{{7*7}}` and checking if 49 appears in the response — a simple arithmetic expression that reveals template rendering. SSRF and XXE are both instances of the server making unintended outbound requests — the defense is the same: allowlist, disable dangerous features at the parser level (disable FEATURE_EXTERNAL_ENTITIES in libxml2).
quote_to_say: Injection is code-data confusion — the defense is always structural separation, not escaping.
